contrastMetadata.transferBoundary: Classify the strategy from its invariant, legal elimination, data access, and workload—not from words such as sorted, threshold, search, left, right, or mid.

items.prompt: A function contains variables named left, right, and mid, but right decreases by one on every iteration. Which classification is justified?

options.text: It has linear worst-case progress unless another branch provides stronger shrinkage.
It is binary search and therefore O(log n).
It is O(1) because only three variables are used.
It is automatically O(n²) because it has two boundaries.

feedback.reason: Classification follows executed updates and invariant, not identifier names.
feedback.details: If the interval loses one candidate per iteration, midpoint syntax does not create logarithmic behavior.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary_by_names: Variable names do not determine how fast the candidate set shrinks.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.constant: Space variables do not determine iteration count.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.quadratic: Two boundaries do not imply nested work.

items.prompt: A problem statement uses the phrase “find a threshold.” What must be proven before choosing binary search?

options.text: The threshold predicate has one directional transition over an ordered candidate domain.
Nothing else; the word threshold is sufficient.
Only that the input contains numbers.
Only that a midpoint can be computed.

feedback.reason: A threshold label must correspond to an actual boundary in predicate truth.
feedback.details: The proof, not the keyword, supplies the half-discard rule.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.word_threshold: Natural-language wording does not guarantee monotonicity.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.numeric_input: Numeric values can support arbitrary conditions.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.midpoint: Every finite range has a midpoint, including invalid search models.

items.prompt: The input is described as sorted, but the query asks for the first value whose hash has an even final bit. Which strategy follows from the description alone?

options.text: No binary-search conclusion follows; the hash condition may require scanning unless separately indexed.
Binary search is mandatory because sorted appears in the prompt.
Use lower bound on the hash parity.
Check only the middle hash because mid implies logarithmic search.

feedback.reason: The sort key and query property must align.
feedback.details: A surface keyword cannot replace a proof that one side is impossible.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary_sorted: The condition need not be monotonic in value order.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.lower_bound_hash: Parity does not form one ordered boundary.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.mid_hash: The midpoint does not classify either side.

items.prompt: A loop is named binarySearch but sequentially checks array[i] for i = 0, 1, 2, .... What is it?

options.text: A linear scan.
Binary search because the function name says so.
A logarithmic hybrid because the name and loop differ.
Impossible to classify even with the loop described.

feedback.reason: Algorithm names are claims that must match the implementation.
feedback.details: Sequentially advancing one index and checking candidates is a scan regardless of the label.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary_search: Behavior, not the function name, defines the algorithm.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hybrid_log: The executed work is sequential candidate inspection.

items.prompt: A loop uses a variable named scanIndex but halves a proven monotonic candidate interval every iteration. Which classification is correct?

options.text: It implements binary-search structure despite the variable name.
It is a linear scan because the variable is called scanIndex.
It is O(1) because there is one index variable.
It is not a search because mid is not named explicitly.

feedback.reason: The invariant and update rule define the algorithm.
feedback.details: A correct half-discard search remains binary search under any sensible identifiers.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.linear_by_name: Names do not override interval-halving semantics.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.constant: The interval requires logarithmically many iterations.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.not_search: A midpoint can be computed under another identifier.

items.prompt: A solution contains a while loop and two pointers. Is that enough to distinguish binary search from a two-pointer scan?

options.text: No. The candidate invariant and pointer updates must be examined.
Yes. Two pointers always mean binary search.
Yes. A while loop always means a scan.
Yes, if the variables are named left and right.

feedback.reason: Shared syntax is not a strategy boundary.
feedback.details: Binary search discards a proven region; a two-pointer scan typically advances one or both endpoints according to a different invariant.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_two_binary: Two-pointer scans also maintain two indices.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_while_scan: Binary search is commonly iterative with while.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_names: Those names appear in both patterns.

items.prompt: A problem says “search in O(log n)” but the input is unsorted and no preprocessing is permitted. What should the analyst do?

options.text: Challenge the stated target or identify missing structure; do not invent an unsound binary search.
Use midpoint discard because the complexity target demands it.
Assume the input is sorted even though it is not stated.
Rename the array to sortedValues.

feedback.reason: Complexity requirements must be reconciled with available structure.
feedback.details: The correct response may be to request a sorted/indexed precondition or explain that the bound is infeasible under the current contract.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.force_binary: A desired bound cannot create correctness conditions.

items.prompt: A code review comment says, “This has nested if statements, so it cannot be binary search.” Which response is correct?

options.text: Nested conditionals do not decide the pattern; interval elimination and progress do.
Any nested conditional forces linear scanning.
Binary search must contain exactly one if statement.
Big-O can be read directly from indentation depth.

feedback.reason: Surface control-flow shape is weaker evidence than the maintained invariant.
feedback.details: A binary search may need multiple branches for equality, ordered-half detection, or boundary semantics.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.if_forces_scan: Branch structure does not imply candidate count.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.complexity_syntax: Executed iterations and work determine complexity.

items.prompt: The requirement says “closest value.” Does that phrase identify binary search by itself?

options.text: No. It depends on sorted order, access model, tie-breaking, and the exact closeness contract.
Yes. Closest always means binary search.
Yes, and it always means linear scan.
Return the first midpoint because it is geometrically central.

feedback.reason: Problem keywords name an output goal, not necessarily the mechanism.
feedback.details: Binary search becomes appropriate when sorted value boundaries locate neighboring candidates whose distances can be compared.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.always_binary: Unsorted data or custom metrics may require other methods.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.always_scan: Sorted numeric data can support a boundary-based closest lookup.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.mid_closest: Index centrality is unrelated to value distance.

items.prompt: A function computes mid = (left + right) // 2 but then recursively explores both halves. Which complexity signal is most accurate?

options.text: Computing mid does not make it logarithmic; exploring both halves may visit the whole domain or more.
Any midpoint recursion is O(log n).
The midpoint calculation is O(1), so the whole function is O(1).
It becomes a scan only if an index is named i.

feedback.reason: The recurrence follows how many subproblems are explored.
feedback.details: Binary search obtains T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1); exploring both halves follows a different recurrence.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.always_log: Logarithmic search explores one retained half, not both.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.linear_scan_name: Names do not determine the recurrence.

items.prompt: A sorted array query uses a helper named isValid(mid), but isValid alternates across indices. Which classification is correct?

options.text: It is an arbitrary-condition scan problem unless another structure is added.
It is monotonic because the helper is called isValid.
Passing mid makes it binary-searchable.
Sorted input alone makes the helper monotonic.

feedback.reason: Semantic behavior must be inspected directly.
feedback.details: A scan can evaluate every index; binary search cannot safely discard a side under an alternating helper.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.monotonic_by_name: The truth sequence, not the name, determines monotonicity.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary_by_parameter: Any index can be passed to an arbitrary predicate.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorted_enough: The helper may not follow the sort key.

items.prompt: Which observations are insufficient by themselves to classify code as binary search? Select all that apply.

options.text: Variables named left, right, and mid.
The prompt contains the word sorted.
The implementation uses a while loop.
Each midpoint outcome proves one whole side impossible and the interval shrinks proportionally.

feedback.reason: Syntax and vocabulary are weak signals.
feedback.details: The half-discard proof and boundary invariant are the decisive evidence.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.left_right_mid: The updates may still be linear or incorrect.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.sorted_word: The query may not align with the order.

items.prompt: Which evidence should replace keyword-based strategy classification? Select all that apply.

options.text: A statement describing where the answer can still remain.
A proof connecting midpoint outcomes to impossible regions.
A demonstration that every nonterminal step shrinks the candidate set.
Identifiers copied from a standard template.

feedback.reason: Classification should rest on semantic and operational evidence.
feedback.details: Familiar names can help readability only after the algorithm is already correct.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.candidate_invariant: This defines the active search state.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.progress_measure: This determines termination and iteration count.

items.prompt: Which code behaviors are compatible with a linear scan despite binary-search-like names? Select all that apply.

options.text: A boundary moves by exactly one position on every iteration.
Every element is eventually inspected in the worst case.
Midpoint outcomes do not imply anything about unchecked regions.
Exactly one proven half is retained after every mismatch.

feedback.reason: The executed candidate progression defines the method.
feedback.details: Names can obscure a scan but cannot change its work or proof structure.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.one_step_boundary: The candidate count may decline linearly.

items.prompt: Which prompt words require further analysis rather than an automatic binary-search choice? Select all that apply.

options.text: sorted.
threshold.
minimum.
A proven false-to-true predicate over a bounded ordered domain.

feedback.reason: Common interview vocabulary identifies hypotheses to test, not guaranteed strategies.
feedback.details: The explicit first-true proof is different because it states the actual transferable mechanism.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.first_true_proof: This phrase already includes the decisive structure rather than a surface keyword.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.sorted: The query still must align with the order.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.threshold: The threshold may not represent a monotonic predicate.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.minimum: Optimization alone does not create monotonic feasibility.

items.prompt: A loop uses variables left, right, and mid but removes exactly one candidate per O(1) iteration. Another loop uses variable i and doubles i each O(1) iteration until i >= n. Select the worst-case time of each.
constraints: The first loop starts with n candidates.
The second loop starts at i = 1.

feedback.reason: Variable names can point in the opposite direction from the true complexity.
feedback.details: The update magnitude and termination condition determine iteration counts.

contrastSets.transferBoundary: Classify the strategy from its invariant, legal elimination, data access, and workload—not from words such as sorted, threshold, search, left, right, or mid.
